narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid LSAT score and upper-middle family income background. However, the admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on academic and background factors from past students, estimated an 84.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam— a threshold that led to your application's rejection to protect the school's outcomes. The biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure were your Year 1 and Year 3 school grade deciles, both ranking in the lowest 10% compared to typical students who average around the middle of their classes. These early and later academic standings stood out as the most influential, signaling to the model a higher risk of bar struggles despite your other qualifications. In contrast, your LSAT score, above the dataset average, worked strongly in your favor by pushing toward a passing prediction. Your undergraduate GPA, slightly below average, and full-time enrollment status offered minor positive nudges, while family income had a small negative pull. Overall, your profile differs from typical admitted students, who tend to have stronger decile rankings in school grades, making those the key hurdles here. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar starts through targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your grade deciles, perhaps seeking tutoring or academic coaching tailored for law prep. You might also explore part-time options or gap-year experiences to strengthen your record. Reapplying with improved academics could shift the model's view dramatically. We're rooting for you to turn this into your success story.
